business_info: Structural linkages in molecular connectivity analysis represent the primary bonding interactions that define atomic connectivity within the molecular framework.

In the legacy QSAR notebooks, "peripheral heteroatoms" were sometimes distinguished from "embedded heteroatoms" based on whether the heteroatom sat on a terminal substituent or within the main scaffold ring system, but this distinction was never formalized in the production schema.

Toxicology screeners occasionally refer to "privileged heteroatom motifs" when a particular N/O/S pattern recurs across multiple high-liability series, but there is no single company-wide definition of what counts as privileged in this context.

For internal reporting, medicinal chemists sometimes describe atoms using position-centric labels such as "5-position" or "19-position" based on enumeration in the original SD files, but these labels do not always align with the numeric suffixes embedded in atom_id.

Some cheminformatics pipelines historically treated any atom whose atom_id ended with "_1" as a pseudo-anchor atom for substructure search templates, but this convention is not enforced in the current catalog and should not be assumed when analyzing connectivity.

Earlier versions of the atom table included a separate column for topological atom index, but this was collapsed into the atom_id string during a schema simplification, so any index-like information must now be parsed from atom_id if needed.

Within exploratory toxicophore mapping, analysts sometimes look at "outer-ring" positions (like 17-, 18-, or 19-position atoms in aromatic systems) to characterize substitution patterns, but there is no formally curated list of which positions are considered outer-ring across all scaffolds.

In internal slide decks, teams may loosely describe certain molecules as "catalog-era" or "post-catalog" compounds based on when they were first screened, but these narrative labels are not tied to specific registration_date cutoffs in the database.

Project teams occasionally segment the screening collection into "waves" (e.g., wave 1, wave 2, legacy wave) based on when batches were ingested, but these informal waves were never backfilled into a dedicated column and can only sometimes be approximated from registration_date and batch identifiers.

Some historical reports referred to an "exploratory cataloging" window during which molecules were registered more manually via SD file drops rather than the current LIMS workflow; this historical term does not correspond to a single, well-documented date range in the database.

Registration waves were initially aligned with major hardware refreshes in the screening labs, so "early" vs "late" series are sometimes talked about relative to those equipment changes rather than strict calendar years.

Chemists occasionally label certain scaffold families as "frontier catalog" compounds in presentations; this is an informal nickname for some of the first series explored by the group and is not encoded in any schema field.

In some internal toxicology dashboards, compounds are binned into qualitative risk bands like "borderline", "concerning", and "severe" toxicity bands for visualization convenience, but these labels are not stored in the molecule table and may correspond to different numeric cutpoints across projects.

A few historical slide decks refer to a "red-list" of problematic molecules based on an early toxicity heuristic; that list was never maintained as a dedicated flag column, and the scoring rule behind it was revised several times.

During exploratory screening, reviewers sometimes referred to "amber" compounds that were not cleanly safe or unsafe; this term loosely aligned with intermediate numeric risk scores but was not strictly defined in the central schema.

Historical risk rating rubrics occasionally used a five-tier scale (A–E) that was later collapsed into the current numeric risk_level field; mappings between old letter grades and numeric bins were done project-by-project rather than through a single global rule.

Some early LIMS exports included both a categorical toxicity label and a separate numeric alert score; as the schema evolved, the alert score became the primary risk_level metric, and textual categories were dropped from routine exports.

In cheminformatics documentation, "structural linkage" is sometimes used more broadly to discuss graph neighborhoods or paths between atoms, not just the immediate bond connecting a pair of atoms, so context is important when interpreting that phrase.

Some internal whitepapers distinguish between "topological linkages" (purely graph-based bonds) and "functional linkages" (bonds considered part of a pharmacophore), but this distinction is conceptual only and not encoded in the bond_type field.

In a few early design documents, the term "primary bonding interaction" was contrasted with looser associations like salt bridges or hydrogen bonds, but only covalent bonds were ever represented in the bond and connectivity tables.

Older cheminformatics scripts sometimes treated aromatic bonds as a distinct linkage class separate from standard single and double bonds, but this nuance was lost when bond_type was simplified to a small set of generic codes.

Some draft SOPs used the phrase "structural linkage count" interchangeably with "degree" of an atom (the number of directly attached neighbors), but other documents used it to mean the total number of bonds of a certain type, so the exact meaning can vary by author.

The connected table was introduced after performance issues were observed when parsing atom pairs directly from the encoded bond_id field; both representations were kept for backward compatibility, so some legacy scripts still derive connectivity from bond_id despite the newer tables.

The atom_bonds table is favored by some cheminformatics staff for graph algorithms because it stores each undirected bond only once, but visualization tools sometimes use connected instead so they can easily traverse from an atom to all neighbors via either atom_id column.

Some early technical notes suggested that atom-level network analysis could be done either from the parsed bond table or from the connected table, with trade-offs in storage vs. lookup simplicity; no single approach was mandated across all analytics code.

For deduplicated connectivity metrics, analysts generally prefer to reference atom_bonds, but tables like connected or bond may be used for more detailed provenance when reconstructing specific SD file exports.

The bond table retains the original encoded bond identifiers from the SD source, which can be useful for audit trails and reproducing external partner datasets, even though downstream connectivity analyses often favor the more normalized connectivity tables.
